images.caption:
Figure 1:Overview.We aim to enhance robotic manipulation by improving articulated part perception from a single RGB-D image.
The core of our approach is a novel affordance representation, GPS, which is easy to scale with high-quality data.
Our model outperforms existing pose-based and flow-based methods in part perception accuracy and manipulation success rate.
Figure 2:VR hardware and interfaces.The headset tracks the fingers and renders the tracking points as red points.
Figure 3:Geometric structure formulation.(a) Part rotation along revolute axis; (b) part translation along prismatic axis.

Figure 4:Hardware settings and annotation pipeline.Before interacting with an object, the annotator places axis{𝐪𝟏,𝐪𝟐}\{\mathbf{q_{1}},\mathbf{q_{2}}\}virtually. During interaction, the part point𝐩\mathbf{p}is attached with fingers. For each object, multiple RGB-D videos with different headset views are recorded.
The annotator begins and ends the recording by performing a pinch gesture with their non-interacting hand.
Figure 5:Dataset overview.Our VR-GPS is diverse and efficient.
Figure 6:Heuristic manipulation policy based on GPS prediction.

Figure 7:Visualization results of robot experiments.Waypoints𝒯𝐆={𝐓t}t=1𝐭\mathcal{T}_{\mathbf{G}}=\{\mathbf{T}_{t}\}_{t=1}^{\mathbf{t}}are depicted in gradient color from blue to purple.
In GPS-GT and GPS, red points denote𝐪^1,𝐪^2{\hat{\mathbf{q}}_{1},\hat{\mathbf{q}}_{2}}, a green point denotes𝐩^\hat{\mathbf{p}}, and cyan grasps marks different initial grasp poses. For Door task in GPS, an additional view is provided to clearly display the otherwise occluded𝐩^\hat{\mathbf{p}}.
In CAPNet, the predicted part bounding boxes are shown in red.
In GFlow, the predicted flow is visualized as a green line, spanning a total of four steps.
In GPS-𝐦^\hat{\mathbf{m}}, the blue points indicate𝐦^\hat{\mathbf{m}}.

Figure 8:Failure cases.The folder is a failure example for GPS-𝐦^\hat{\mathbf{m}}, and the bucket and drawer are failure examples for GPS.
Red points are{𝐪^1,𝐪^2}\{\hat{\mathbf{q}}_{1},\hat{\mathbf{q}}_{2}\}, green points are𝐩^\hat{\mathbf{p}}, and blue points are𝐦^\hat{\mathbf{m}}.
Figure 9:Failure cases for post-processing methods RSRD[18]and ArtGS[24], which are prone to errors.
Figure 10:A large proportion of current robot tasks are related to object geometric structure. Tasks with precise force control,e.g., hit a pool ball, are out of scope of this work.
Figure 11:Object part pose and corresponding GPS.
Figure 12:The test objects in real robot experiments. We show a random view for each object.
